stories.headline: Update: Suspect in downtown Denver scooter shooting posts bond, victim says he no longer feels safe
stories.summary:
Nico Francis, the man shot in the neck Friday night near 18th and Stout streets in downtown Denver in what police described as an apparent random act of violence, says he is gripped by anxiety after learning the suspect posted bond before Francis was even discharged from the hospital. Anthony Apodaca, 31, was charged with first-degree attempted murder. Prosecutors requested a $100,000 cash-only bond, but a judge instead set bond at $75,000 with home confinement, GPS monitoring and a requirement to surrender firearms.

Francis told 9NEWS that the shooting occurred while he was riding a scooter to meet friends. He said a passenger in a white SUV yelled at people along the sidewalk, then turned his attention to Francis and pulled out a gun. When Francis encountered the same vehicle at a stoplight, he said he tried to de-escalate the situation before Apodaca fired, with the bullet passing through his neck and exiting through his back shoulder. Doctors at Denver Health told him the bullet missed major arteries by millimeters.

"I don't feel safe and it's adding a lot to my anxiety," Francis said. "I don't know that I can feel safe until there is accountability." He added that he feels there is "a trend of not taking crimes like this very seriously." One bright spot, he said, was a person experiencing homelessness who rushed to apply pressure to his wound and helped him call 911. "He saved my life," Francis said.

stories.headline: Man shot by Denver police after refusing to drop weapon dies from injuries
stories.summary:
A man shot by Denver police officers Tuesday afternoon has died from his injuries, the Denver Police Department confirmed. Officers were initially dispatched around 3:41 p.m. to a report of a man threatening others with a gun near Lakewood-Dry Gulch Park in west Denver. Police Chief Ron Thomas said the man refused commands to drop his weapon and continued advancing toward officers before he was shot.

The shooting is now being investigated as a police use-of-force incident resulting in death. No officers were reported injured during the confrontation. The identity of the man who died has not yet been publicly released pending family notification.

The incident adds to ongoing scrutiny of Denver police use-of-force cases. Department protocol calls for an independent review of all officer-involved shootings. The Denver Office of the Independent Monitor is expected to conduct a parallel review.

stories.headline: Update: Goat Trail Fire near Boulder fully contained; second wildfire briefly prompts evacuations in Larimer County
stories.summary:
The Goat Trail Fire that broke out early Wednesday morning west of Boulder reached 100% containment by approximately 9:30 a.m., according to Boulder Fire Rescue. The blaze, which started roughly 500 feet west of Hawthorne Avenue near 4th Street, was initially estimated at 2 to 3 acres but drone mapping revised the size to just under 2 acres. An evacuation warning issued before 4 a.m. for areas west of the 200 block of Hawthorne Avenue was lifted by 8:30 a.m.

A second fire, the Cougar Run fire in Larimer County south of Carter Lake, also prompted mandatory and voluntary evacuations along County Road 31 beginning around 6:50 a.m. Wednesday. The blaze was initially estimated at about 10 acres but was later reduced to an estimate of 3.5 acres. Loveland Fire, Berthoud Fire and the Colorado Division of Fire Prevention and Control assisted on scene, and all evacuation orders were lifted before 10:30 a.m.

Firefighters remained on scene at both locations through the day to monitor conditions amid forecasts for strong afternoon winds. The Open Space Mountain Park's Goat Trail remained closed, and residents were asked to avoid the area. The twin fires underscore ongoing concerns about Colorado's fire risk during an extremely low-snowpack spring.

stories.headline: Denver Water approves drought pricing for first time since 2013 as snowpack crisis deepens
stories.summary:
The Denver Board of Water Commissioners voted Wednesday to implement temporary drought pricing, marking the first time such a measure has been enacted since 2013. The decision comes as Colorado's statewide snowpack sits at just 24% of normal, with headwaters in Summit County only slightly higher at 27%. A recent storm provided a marginal boost but was insufficient to alter the broader outlook, Denver Water officials said.

Under drought pricing, customers who use water above established thresholds will face higher tiered rates designed to discourage excessive consumption. The tier 1 base rate for Denver proper is $3.02 per unit for 2026. Denver Water has also already implemented mandatory outdoor watering restrictions that limit residential outdoor use to twice per week, with days determined by address number. Restaurants are required to serve water only upon customer request.

Denver Parks and Recreation said there is no guarantee that Garfield and Huston lakes will be refilled this season without significant improvement in precipitation and snowpack. The water shortage is affecting communities across the Front Range, with Aurora having already declared a Stage 1 water shortage and Brighton adopting its own drought restrictions targeting a 20% reduction in use.

stories.headline: Half of Colorado's 64 counties lost population last year, including Denver, Arapahoe and Boulder
stories.summary:
More than half of Colorado's 64 counties — 33 in total — lost population last year, including four of the state's largest: Arapahoe, Denver, Boulder and Pueblo counties, according to new U.S. Census Bureau data analyzed by The Denver Post. Colorado added only 24,059 residents last year, its slowest pace of population growth since the late 1980s. Arapahoe County recorded the largest raw population loss in the state.

Much of the state's remaining growth is now concentrated in a corridor stretching from Weld County south to Elbert and El Paso counties. Resort counties, historically among Colorado's most attractive destinations, are beginning to shrink alongside the aging agricultural communities of the Eastern Plains. More than a third of the state's counties now have fewer residents than they did in 2020, including Jefferson County, the state's fourth most populous.

Immigration has accounted for the majority of Colorado's population gains this decade, contributing 130,218 of the 225,688 new residents added since 2020. However, state demographers warn that international immigration is already declining sharply under federal policy changes and may produce negative net flows in 2026. Rural Jackson County has suffered the steepest five-year percentage decline, losing 12.2% of its population since 2020, leaving it with just 1,211 residents.

stories.headline: Denver Zoo sues design firm and contractor over defects in $19 million sea lion habitat
stories.summary:
The Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance has filed a lawsuit in Denver District Court against Colorado companies Vertix Builders and Barker Rinker Seacast Architecture, alleging dozens of defects in the $19 million Schlessman Shores sea lion habitat that opened in June 2025. The zoo says the facility is already rusting, leaking and experiencing mechanical failures less than a year after its debut. The lawsuit, filed March 17, seeks unspecified damages on claims of breach of contract and breach of warranty against both firms.

Among the specific problems cited, the zoo says BRS's design failures led to construction delays and required costly revisions to the pool, curbs, skimmers and beach area, as well as failures in the sump pump design and inappropriate metal hardware specifications that have resulted in widespread rust. Vertix is accused of delivering work that produced broken sump pumps, water leakage, a falling pipe, exposed metal ties, peeling paint, faulty caulking and saltwater leaching from the upper pool into the moat.

The zoo says it notified both firms of the deficiencies and neither took adequate steps to resolve them. The habitat was designed to save 8 million gallons of water per year and replace the zoo's 1987-era Northern Shores facility. During the two-year construction period, the zoo's five California sea lions were housed elsewhere. "We are committed to resolving this matter as efficiently as possible," the zoo said in a statement, adding it aims to ensure the habitat "stands the test of time."

stories.headline: Denver Mayor Johnston names homelessness policy adviser Cole Chandler to lead city housing department
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston has appointed Cole Chandler, a policy adviser who played a central role in the mayor's homelessness reduction campaign, as the new executive director of the Department of Housing Stability. Chandler has served since Johnston took office as the mayor's primary representative on homelessness matters before the Denver City Council. His appointment places a close Johnston ally in charge of the city's largest housing and shelter initiatives.

The appointment comes as Denver continues to grapple with homelessness and an acute affordable housing shortage. Johnston's administration has made reducing street-level homelessness a signature policy priority, and Chandler's elevation signals continuity in that approach. No details about Chandler's salary or formal start date were immediately released.

The Department of Housing Stability oversees shelter contracts, affordable housing funding and a range of supportive services for Denver residents experiencing or at risk of homelessness. Chandler's background is in policy rather than housing administration, a distinction that may draw scrutiny as the department manages complex contracts and a significant municipal budget.

stories.headline: RTD chief Debra Johnson to step down at end of contract
stories.summary:
Debra Johnson, the chief executive of the Regional Transportation District, announced she will depart the agency at the conclusion of her current contract, RTD confirmed to The Denver Gazette. Johnson joined RTD in November 2020 after serving as deputy CEO of Long Beach Transit and previously held roles at transit agencies in Los Angeles, San Francisco and Washington, D.C. She earns a $315,000 annual salary.

RTD board chair Patrick O'Keefe praised Johnson's tenure in a statement, saying "the agency is in a much better place because of her dedication" and citing improvements in safety and security among her major accomplishments. Her departure will open a leadership search at an agency that continues to face significant operational and financial challenges, including a long-term light rail and commuter rail expansion plan that critics say no longer meets the modern needs of the Denver metro area.

Johnson's exit comes as RTD navigates debates over route cuts, the future of the Colorado Connector passenger rail project and a broader reckoning over the agency's service model. Transit advocates and elected officials have called on RTD to refocus its priorities on core ridership needs rather than legacy expansion commitments.

stories.headline: Colorado Auto Show, inaugural Denver Jazz Festival headline weekend events in the metro
stories.summary:
The Colorado Auto Show returns to the Colorado Convention Center this weekend, running Friday through Sunday with hours from 10 a.m. to 9 p.m. on Friday and Saturday and 10 a.m. to 6 p.m. on Sunday. Admission is $16 for adults 17 and older, with children 16 and under admitted free. Attendees can view new models, take test drives and try race simulators across a wide range of brands.

Denver's inaugural Jazz Festival will also take place Thursday through Sunday at venues across Denver and Boulder, with a concentration of performances in the historic Five Points cultural district. The festival features local musicians and includes several free shows, among them a performance by the Daryl Gott Trio at Union Station. Ticket prices vary by show.

Additional weekend highlights include the Rocky Mountain Records Show, offering more than 150,000 records from vendors nationwide at an admission price starting at $7, and the Masters Mini Bar Crawl on Tennyson Street, a six-hole mini golf pub crawl benefiting the Colorado State Beekeepers Association for $20 per participant.

stories.headline: Avalanche clinch Central Division title and Western Conference top seed with 3-1 win over Blues
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche clinched the Central Division championship and secured the No. 1 seed in the Western Conference with a 3-1 victory over the St. Louis Blues on Tuesday night. Valeri Nichushkin scored twice in the win. The accomplishment came eight years to the day that Gabe Landeskog and Nathan MacKinnon helped the team return to the playoffs in a winner-take-all Game 82 against the same Blues franchise.

Despite the milestone, coach Jared Bednar was measured in his reaction, noting the team still needs one additional win to clinch the best overall record in the NHL and secure home-ice advantage throughout the playoffs. "We're not throwing parades or that kind of stuff yet," Bednar said. "We should celebrate it a little bit, because it's a big goal of ours to start the year and we've accomplished part of it."

The Avalanche had posted a historic 31-2-7 start to the season but went 20-14-3 thereafter, hampered by a string of injuries. The team has been without multiple top-eight skaters at various points, posting a 9-10-3 record in those circumstances. Separately, Bednar confirmed that defenseman Cale Makar will miss time with an upper-body injury, adding a note of caution heading into the playoff stretch.

stories.headline: Rockies beat Astros 5-1 for second series win of season, a mark not reached until June in 2025
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies defeated the Houston Astros 5-1 Tuesday night at Coors Field, recording their fifth win of the 2026 season in just 11 games and securing their second series victory of the year. In 2025, the Rockies did not record their second series win until June 1, making the early-season achievement a notable benchmark for a franchise coming off one of its worst campaigns in recent memory.

Kyle Freeland started on the mound, striking out five batters and allowing just one run over 5.1 innings. Antonio Senzatela closed out the game with 2.2 innings of hitless relief, striking out three. Offensively, Willi Castro and Mickey Moniak each hit two-run home runs to power the Colorado attack, while the team collected 10 hits overall.

The win extended Colorado's winning streak to three games and left the club in second place in the NL West through the early portion of the schedule. The Rockies can reach .500 with a fourth consecutive victory. New additions to the roster, including veterans Castro and Moniak and younger players such as T.J. Rumfield, have drawn attention for their contributions to the team's stronger-than-expected start.

stories.headline: Broncos mock draft roundup: Analysts project linebacker, tight end and defensive line as top priorities
stories.summary:
With the 2026 NFL Draft roughly two weeks away, national draft analysts are projecting Colorado positions of need across multiple rounds for the Denver Broncos. The team no longer holds a first-round pick after acquiring wide receiver Jaylen Waddle in March but retains seven total picks. ESPN's Matt Miller projects Denver to select Missouri linebacker Josiah Trotter in the second round, calling him "a downhill thumper" with "NFL bloodlines" who can impact the run game immediately.

Other analysts see different needs at the top of Denver's board. Sporting News projects tight end Eli Stowers of Vanderbilt, while CBS Sports has Denver taking Ohio State cornerback Davison Igbinosun. Multiple analysts in later rounds project tight ends, with Ohio State's Max Klare and Stanford's Sam Roush among the names mentioned. Defensive linemen from Texas A&M, South Carolina and Missouri also appear across multiple projections.

The draft comes as the Broncos seek to build around second-year quarterback Bo Nix following a promising 2025 season. General Manager George Paton and the front office met with media at the NFL Annual Meeting last week, where the team's on-field improvement and draft strategy were discussed. The draft is scheduled to begin in approximately two weeks.

weather.notable: About 13 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I think you should be a child for as long as you can. I have been successful for 74 years being a child.

topStory.headline: U.S.-Brokered Iran Ceasefire Leaves Lebanon Fighting as Beirut Strikes Kill at Least 182
topStory.summary:
The United States, Israel, and Iran reached a tentative 14-day ceasefire Wednesday, halting nearly six weeks of direct military conflict, but the agreement immediately fractured over competing interpretations of its scope and terms. President Donald Trump announced the pause on social media, saying the U.S. had met all military objectives and was moving toward a longer-term peace agreement, walking back earlier threats to destroy Iranian infrastructure and, in his words, Iranian "civilization."

The ceasefire's fragility became apparent within hours of its announcement. Iran insisted it would retain control of the Strait of Hormuz and continue uranium enrichment, positions the Trump administration disputed, and Tehran accused Washington of major violations. Pakistan, which served as a key mediator alongside China, said the ceasefire extended to Lebanon, but Israel and Trump rejected that interpretation, with Trump calling the continued Israeli campaign in Lebanon "a separate skirmish."

Israel launched what its military described as its largest coordinated strike of the current war within hours of the ceasefire announcement, hitting more than 100 Hezbollah targets in Beirut, southern Lebanon, and the Bekaa Valley within a 10-minute window. Lebanese officials said at least 182 people were killed and hundreds more wounded, making it the deadliest single day of the Israel-Hezbollah conflict. Associated Press journalists reported charred bodies at one of Beirut's busiest intersections in the central Corniche al Mazraa neighborhood, a densely populated commercial and residential area.

The strikes gutted what brief sense of relief had settled over Lebanon after news of the ceasefire broke. Apartment buildings were hit without warning on a clear afternoon, and large numbers of war-displaced people who had sheltered in Beirut were caught in the bombardment. The Lebanese government has not been a party to the U.S.-Iran negotiations, and Hezbollah's status under any eventual agreement remains unresolved.

The 14-day window is intended to allow negotiations toward a permanent settlement between Washington and Tehran, but the first day exposed deep disagreements over what the ceasefire covers, who enforces it, and whether Iran's nuclear and maritime policies are subject to the deal. Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Vice President JD Vance were both visible in diplomatic settings Wednesday, signaling continued high-level U.S. engagement, while the situation in Lebanon remained a live conflict with no separate agreement in place.
